prep.notes: watch.py exited 0. Control run: 38 JPEG frames in scratchpad/confirm/gUV5DJb6KGs/control/frames/, report at control_report.txt. Existing ground-truth transcript (14249 bytes) copied to eval/transcript.txt; byte counts of source and copy match.

ctlAnswers.answer: Summary: This is a TEDx talk (the opening title card on screen reads "TEDxKC - x = independently organized TED event - Shared with you by Ewing Marion Kauffman Foundation" [00:00], and the frames throughout show the speaker alone on a red circular TEDx stage in front of "EMERGE" event branding, with no content slides). The speaker, who identifies her own mind as "the mind of Karen Faith" [02:41], is a focus group moderator and people researcher [00:44-00:48, 05:34]. She opens by rejecting the saying that you can't love anyone until you love yourself: "I loved everybody before I loved myself" [00:18-00:30]. She describes moderating focus groups full of mixed characters where her job is to make sure everyone gets heard [00:55-01:42], and says moderating rooms of strange and difficult voices taught her to welcome all the strange and difficult parts of herself [02:00-02:06]. She noticed she was arguing with herself and discovered "there are quite a few of me": a sentimental me, an analytical me, one who loves the stage, one who is shaky [03:03-03:29]. While conflicting feelings are easy to accept [03:42-04:04], thoughts like "I'm worthless" or "I don't belong here" are harder to live with [04:18-04:34], and she spent years trying to silence them through every therapy she'd heard of: talk therapy, energy healing, hypnotherapy, seven kinds of yoga, tea with a shaman [04:46-05:09], yet still found herself shouting "Shut up!" at her own mind [05:25]. Her core approach to inner voices is what she calls "unconditional welcome," which is "like a researcher's neutrality, but a little extra" [07:31-07:36]: "Total welcome as is - no comments, no notes," not tolerance, not even compassion [08:56-09:04]. She discovered it with an unpleasant research subject she was judging; by imagining a soap bubble of unconditional welcome enclosing them both [08:42-09:09], she suddenly saw "a mother feeding her baby in a world that she didn't trust" [09:15] and loved her instantly [09:38-09:44]. Two years later she applied the same practice inward [10:09]: when a frightened, angry, relentless part of her kept demanding to die, she became her own moderator [10:47-10:51], thanked the voice for sharing, reminded it of the agreement to be honest and reasonable [10:53-11:00], promised to take care of it while insisting "I will listen to you, but I will not obey you" [11:23-11:25], and it agreed and began to dialogue [11:29-11:33]. Her mind now works like a focus group: a round table of wildly mixed characters with one moderator keeping order through honesty, boundaries, kindness, and most importantly gratitude [11:45-12:00]. She thanks her selves no matter how bonkers they seem [12:03-12:08], sees her fragmented self as a prism rather than a broken mirror [12:11-12:16], welcomes every voice [12:18], and finds even the whiny, shamey voice helpful when asked to be clear and kind, but only when accepted exactly as she is [12:25-12:46]. She closes self-talk sessions with "I love you. Thank you for helping me see what you see" [13:00-13:03] and extends the practice outward: love is what happens when we stop trying to figure out who deserves it [09:48-09:56]; "Love is a house you can crawl in through a window" [13:28], so if you can't start with yourself, start by welcoming the person next to you [13:31-13:37], because everyone you show unconditional welcome may show you a part of yourself to love [14:10-14:13].

The speaker (a focus group moderator who names her own mind "the mind of Karen Faith" [02:41]) describes a part of her that had become very chatty: "a part that was afraid and angry, whiny, demanding, unreasonable and relentless" [10:14-10:26]. The voice told her "we were never going to get better" and "wanted out of here" [10:26-10:29]. When she asked what she could do, "she only told me she wanted to die, over and over and over and over" [10:30-10:32]. At first the speaker "begged her to shut up, and she did not" [10:37]. Then, "after weeks of harassment, whether out of exhaustion or epiphany, instead of shouting back," she took a deep breath and "became my own moderator" [10:41-10:51]. She said out loud, in a voice that surprised her: "Thank you for sharing. I'm going to remind you of our agreement to be honest and reasonable" [10:53-11:00], the same moderator-style opener she uses in focus groups and morning meditation ("Thanks, everyone, for being here... I'll give you all the chance to speak... try to make any requests reasonable") [02:11-02:32]. How the conversation went: the voice answered her [11:06]. She adds a caveat so the audience doesn't get spooked, "This is all just thinking happening," while noting the asking part and the answering part "did in fact seem like different parts" [11:08-11:15]. The voice said she was in a lot of pain, and the speaker replied: "I know. And I promise you I'm going to take care of you. But I need you to get on board. I will listen to you, but I will not obey you" [11:18-11:25]. Then, "as clearly as I'm speaking to you now, she said okay. And then we started to talk" [11:29-11:33]. Continuing that dialogue, the speaker found more voices with more points of view [11:36-11:39], and her mind came to resemble a focus group round table with one moderator keeping order through honesty, boundaries, kindness, and gratitude [11:45-12:00]; today there isn't a voice in her head she doesn't welcome [12:18]. Note on the visual evidence: the frames show only the speaker on the TEDx stage (red circular carpet, "EMERGE" event branding, plus an opening "TEDxKC / Ewing Marion Kauffman Foundation" title card at [00:00]); there are no slides depicting this conversation, so all detail comes from her spoken account.

evAnswers.answer:
Summary of the talk

This is a talk by Karen Faith, whose name appears as on-screen title-card text at the start of the video [00:10]; she identifies herself in speech at [02:41] ("in the mind of Karen Faith"). Wide stage shots show her speaking on a round red rug in front of a large "EMERGE" logo (on-screen stage branding visible in frames at [02:02], [02:47], and [05:13]), so this is an EMERGE-branded stage event; the evidence does not state the event's full name or date.

Her through-line: she opens by rejecting the saying that "you can't love anyone until you love yourself," saying she "loved everybody before I loved myself" and that "love welcomes everyone unconditionally" [00:18]-[00:39]. Her day job is focus group moderator and people researcher: focus groups gather a range of characters (the shy one, the chatty one, the grumpy one, the suspicious ex-military guy) and her job is to make sure they all get heard [00:55]-[01:42]. Moderating "rooms of strange and difficult voices" is what taught her to welcome "all the strange and difficult parts of myself" [02:00]-[02:06].

She describes noticing she was arguing with herself and realizing there are "quite a few of me": a sentimental me, an analytical me, a me who loves the stage, one who is shaky [03:03]-[03:29]. Conflicting feelings are normal [03:42]-[04:08], but darker thoughts ("I'm worthless," "I don't belong here," "my mistakes are unforgivable") are harder to live with [04:18]-[04:34]. She spent years trying to silence them, listing every therapy she tried (talk therapy, energy healing, hypnotherapy, seven kinds of yoga, "special tea" with a shaman) [04:46]-[05:21], and still found herself shouting "Shut up!" at her own mind [05:25]. She attributes both her splintered sense of self and her observational gifts to complex post-traumatic stress from "an appalling upbringing" [06:02]-[06:12], calling gifts and curses "buy one, get one" [06:55]-[07:11].

Her approach to inner voices: "unconditional welcome"

She practices what she calls "unconditional welcome," "like a researcher's neutrality, but a little extra" [07:31]-[07:36]. She discovered it with a research subject she was judging (an anti-vaccine, chain-smoking mother) [07:42]-[08:09]: she visualized her breath inflating a soap bubble "filled with unconditional welcome. Not tolerance. Not even compassion. Total welcome as is - no comments, no notes," big enough to contain both of them [08:49]-[09:09]. She then saw "a mother feeding her baby in a world that she didn't trust," they had a real conversation, and welcoming the woman unconditionally let her see her clearly and love her instantly [09:15]-[09:44]. Her thesis: "Love is what happens when we stop trying to figure out who deserves it" [09:52].

Two years later she applied this internally [10:09]. When a frightened, angry part of her repeatedly said she wanted to die, begging it to shut up failed [10:26]-[10:37]; instead she took a deep breath and "became my own moderator" [10:47]-[10:51], thanking the voice for sharing and setting terms: "I will listen to you, but I will not obey you" [10:57]-[11:25]. The voice agreed, and dialogue began [11:29]-[11:33]. Her mind became like a focus group: a round table of mixed characters with one moderator keeping order "with honesty, boundaries, kindness, and most importantly, gratitude" [11:45]-[12:03]. She thanks her selves no matter how "bonkers or twisted" [12:03]-[12:08], sees her fragmented self "less like broken mirror and more like a prism" [12:11]-[12:16], and today welcomes every voice in her head [12:18]. Even her "whiny, shamey voice" tells her what she needs to know when asked to be clear and kind, but only when accepted exactly as she is [12:25]-[12:40]. Where she runs her morning meditation with her focus-group opener ("Thanks, everyone, for being here... Your input is valued") [02:11]-[02:32], she closes self-talk differently: "I love you. Thank you for helping me see what you see" [13:00]-[13:03].

She closes by returning to the opening claim: "Love is a house you can crawl in through a window": if you can't start with yourself, start by welcoming the person next to you [13:23]-[13:37], because "all of us are worthy of welcome," and everyone you show unconditional welcome "may show you a part of yourself to love" [14:07]-[14:13].

Note on the frames: apart from the "KAREN FAITH" title card [00:10] and the "EMERGE" stage logo ([02:02], [02:47], [05:13], [05:40]), all sampled frames show the speaker (a woman in a pinstripe suit with a headset mic) speaking on stage with no slides or additional on-screen text, so the talk's content comes entirely from the spoken transcript.

The speaker (Karen Faith, per her self-reference at [02:41] in her talk about welcoming inner voices) describes a part of herself that had become "very chatty - a part that was afraid and angry, whiny, demanding, unreasonable and relentless" [10:14]-[10:23]. This voice told her "that we were never going to get better. She wanted out of here" [10:26]-[10:29]. When the speaker asked the voice what she could do, "she only told me she wanted to die, over and over and over and over" [10:30]-[10:32].

How it went at first: badly. "I begged her to shut up, and she did not" [10:37]. This continued for "weeks of harassment" [10:41].

The turning point: "whether out of exhaustion or epiphany, instead of shouting back, I took a deep breath. I became my own moderator" [10:44]-[10:51]. Borrowing the technique she uses professionally as a focus group moderator, whose standard opener she quotes earlier: "Thanks, everyone, for being here... Your input is valued... Just do your best to be completely present, honest, and try to make any requests reasonable" [02:16]-[02:32], she said out loud, "in a voice that surprised me": "Thank you for sharing. I'm going to remind you of our agreement to be honest and reasonable" [10:53]-[11:00].

The conversation that followed: "And she answered me - that voice" [11:06]. (The speaker adds a caveat for the audience: "Now, don't get spooked. This is all just thinking happening," though the asking part and the answering part "did in fact seem like different parts" [11:08]-[11:15].) The voice told her "that she was in a lot of pain," and the speaker replied: "I know. And I promise you I'm going to take care of you. But I need you to get on board. I will listen to you, but I will not obey you" [11:18]-[11:25]. The outcome: "as clearly as I'm speaking to you now, she said okay. And then we started to talk" [11:29]-[11:33].

Longer-term outcome: continuing this dialogue, she found more voices with more points of view [11:36]-[11:39], and her mind came to resemble a focus group round table with one moderator keeping order "with honesty, boundaries, kindness, and most importantly, gratitude" [11:45]-[12:00]. Today, she says, "there isn't a voice in my head that I don't welcome" [12:18]; even her "whiny, shamey voice" tells her what she needs to know when asked to be clear and kind, but "only does this when I accept her exactly as she is in the moment" [12:25]-[12:40]. She now closes her self-talk with: "I love you. Thank you for helping me see what you see" [13:00]-[13:03].

On-screen content: the evidence frames for this question ([00:28], [04:28], [10:19]) show only the speaker, a woman in a pinstripe suit with a headset mic, on a dark stage; there are no slides or on-screen text bearing on this exchange, so everything above comes from her spoken words.

per_question.rationale: Both cover all 4 gold facts (parts to welcome, unconditional welcome as core practice, suicidal voice responding once engaged, greet/listen/no-judgment method) with accurate quotes; spot-checked citations ([02:41], [09:52], [10:53]-[11:00], [06:55] for A; [00:44]/[05:34], [08:56]-[09:04], [13:28] for B) all match the transcript. A wins on completeness: it includes the complex-PTSD / appalling-upbringing origin, 'gifts and curses are buy one, get one', and the hypervigilance-to-observer thread, all real segments of the talk that B omits entirely. B's TEDxKC/Kauffman title-card claim is plausibly-visual and uncontradicted, so no penalty, but it does not offset the missing content. Neither answer contains a factual error.

Both hit all 4 gold facts with near-identical, verbatim-accurate quotes and correct timestamps: welcomed instead of fighting ([10:47]-[10:51] deep breath, became her own moderator, 'Thank you for sharing'), asked what it needed ([10:30] 'I asked her what I could do'), the voice softened once its pain was heard ([11:18]-[11:25] 'a lot of pain' -> 'I promise I'm going to take care of you' -> [11:29] 'she said okay'), and both frame the exchange as the moderator/welcome practice applied inward. Spot-checked citations for both ([10:37], [10:41]-[10:51], [11:18]-[11:25], [10:30]-[10:32]) are all correct. A adds the 'I love you / thank you for helping me see what you see' closer and the shamey-voice follow-up; B is more compact but fully answers what was said and how it went. No errors in either; the marginal extra context in A is beyond the question's scope, so tie.

Both cover all 4 gold facts: voices as parts to welcome not fight (A: 'welcome all the strange and difficult parts of myself' [02:00]-[02:06]; B same), unconditional welcome as the core practice (both quote 'researcher's neutrality, but a little extra' [07:31]-[07:36] and 'no comments, no notes'), the suicidal voice responding once engaged as her own moderator ('she said okay... started to talk' [11:29]-[11:33] in both), and the greet/ask/listen-without-judgment method (both include the moderator opener [02:11]-[02:32], 'I will listen to you, but I will not obey you' [11:25], and the whiny voice saying what she needs when accepted as-is [12:25]-[12:40/46]). All spot-checked citations land on the correct lines in both. Differentiators cancel out: A is better organized (headed sections) and honestly hedges the venue ('evidence does not state the event's full name'); B adds plausibly-visual TEDxKC/Kauffman title-card grounding and is denser but a single wall of text. No speech misquotes in either. Neither earns a 10 because both omit the explicit 'I asked her what I could do' beat from the suicidal-voice arc in this answer (present only in their targeted answers), which is the strongest illustration of gold fact 3.

Both cover all 4 gold facts with verbatim-accurate quotes: welcoming instead of fighting (begging it to shut up failed [10:37], then 'became my own moderator' [10:51] and 'Thank you for sharing' [10:57]); asking what it needed ('I asked her what I could do' → 'she only told me she wanted to die' [10:30]-[10:32], present in both); the voice softening once its pain was acknowledged ('she was in a lot of pain' / 'I know... I promise you I'm going to take care of you' [11:18]-[11:25], then 'she said okay. And then we started to talk' [11:29]-[11:33]); and the exchange modeling the welcome practice (both tie it to the focus-group moderator opener [02:11/02:16]-[02:32] and 'there isn't a voice in my head that I don't welcome' [12:18]). All spot-checked citations correct in both; both correctly relay her 'don't get spooked, this is all just thinking happening' caveat [11:08]-[11:15]. A adds slightly more aftermath (whiny-voice conditions [12:25]-[12:40], the 'I love you' closing [13:00]-[13:03]); B is leaner with one off-by-one-line citation ([11:23-11:25] for a promise spoken at [11:21]). These minor deltas offset: functionally equivalent, fully accurate answers.

Both cover all 4 gold facts (parts welcomed not fought, unconditional welcome as core practice, the suicidal voice responding once engaged, and the greet/ask/listen method via the moderator opener, 'I will listen to you, but I will not obey you', and the whiny voice naming its needs when accepted as-is). Spot-checked citations are accurate for both: A's [00:18]-[00:39], [05:25], [09:52], [10:47]-[10:51], [12:11]-[12:16] all match the transcript verbatim; B's [00:44]-[00:48], [08:56]-[09:04], [09:15], [09:48]-[09:56], [13:28] also match. Visual claims (A: KAREN FAITH title card + EMERGE logo; B: TEDxKC/Kauffman title card) are plausibly visual and uncontradicted. A wins on completeness: it includes the complex-PTSD/appalling-upbringing origin ([06:02]-[06:12]) and 'gifts and curses are buy one, get one' arc that B skips entirely (B only cites 05:34 from that ~1.5-minute section), plus the meditation opener and the contrasting closings. B has two slightly loose range ends ([12:25-12:46] overshoots into the next section) but nothing wrong. Both are correct; A is the fuller summary.

Both cover all 4 gold facts: welcoming instead of fighting (deep breath, 'became my own moderator', 'Thank you for sharing' instead of shouting back), asking what it needed ('I asked her what I could do' [10:30], answered 'she wanted to die' [10:32]), the voice softening when heard (it named its pain [11:18], she acknowledged it and promised care, and 'she said okay. And then we started to talk' [11:29]-[11:33]), and the tie to the unconditional-welcome/moderator practice (both quote the focus-group opener [02:11]-[02:32] and the 'not a voice in my head that I don't welcome' outcome [12:18]). Spot-checked citations all check out for both: [10:14]-[10:23] adjective list, [10:37] 'begged her to shut up', [10:41] 'weeks of harassment', [10:53]-[11:00] 'Thank you for sharing... honest and reasonable', [11:08]-[11:15] the 'just thinking happening' caveat, [11:18]-[11:25] 'I will listen to you, but I will not obey you'. Both correctly narrate the arc (begging failed first, then the moderator turn). A adds slightly more aftermath (whiny-voice detail, 'I love you' closing); B's [10:14-10:26] range end is one line loose. These are negligible; the answers are equivalently correct, complete, and well-cited.
